definition: A saved setup that lets MySQL Workbench connect to your database.
A place where related data is stored.
A way of organizing data into tables that can be linked together.
A column that gives each row its own unique ID.
A SQL command used to read or change data.
In this guide, a schema is your database: the place that holds your tables.
A set of data arranged in rows and columns.
A number a program uses to connect to a service. MySQL usually uses 3306.
The area in MySQL Workbench where you write and run SQL commands.
The table at the bottom of Workbench that shows query results.
One full line of data in a table.
A vertical section in a table that stores one kind of data.
A data type for whole numbers, such as 1, 25, or 300.
A data type for text.
A data type for dates in the format YYYY-MM-DD.
A setting that means a column cannot be left empty.
A setting that automatically gives each new row the next number.
One box in a table where a row and column meet.
The name or address of the computer MySQL Workbench connects to.
Your own computer.
A saved set of SQL code written together in one file or editor tab, usually run as a group.
